steps.title: Link exposure to distinction
steps.content: The team's conclusion is that the amount of previous language exposure may influence a dog's ability to distinguish a familiar language from an unfamiliar one. A supporting finding must therefore connect greater exposure with a stronger familiar-versus-unfamiliar brain-activity difference.

steps.title: Reject the wrong comparisons
steps.content: Choice A shows dogs of all ages respond more to the familiar language, but it does not show that more exposure creates a stronger distinction. Choice C compares scrambled recordings with the unfamiliar language. Choice D compares familiar language with scrambled recordings. Neither comparison tests familiar versus unfamiliar language.

steps.title: Use the age exposure pattern
steps.content: Older dogs would generally have received more exposure to the language they know. If the difference between familiar-language and unfamiliar-language brain activity was greatest among older dogs, that directly supports the idea that previous exposure influences language distinction ability. The answer is B.
